questions.text: In procurement fraud, what is the primary characteristic of 'phantom billing'?
options: A. Invoicing for services or goods that were never delivered or performed
B. Billing at inflated rates that exceed the contracted price by more than 50%
C. Submitting multiple invoices for the same work to different cost centers
D. Creating fake vendor accounts to process payments through shell companies

questions.text: Which practice is defined as deliberately excluding qualified bidders from the competitive bidding process to reduce competition?
options: A. Bid rigging
B. Bid suppression
C. Competitor exclusion
D. Market allocation

questions.text: What does 'front-end loading' refer to in the context of government contract fraud?
options: A. Accelerating the procurement timeline to bypass competitive bidding requirements
B. Inflating costs in early contract phases with the intention to reduce costs in later phases or claim cost overruns
C. Requiring advance payments before contract performance begins
D. Distributing contract work among multiple subcontractors upfront

questions.text: In procurement fraud terminology, what is 'specification gaming'?
options: A. Using contract specifications to gain competitive advantage through superior performance
B. Writing contract specifications that appear neutral but technically exclude all competitors except one
C. Modifying contract specifications after bid submission to favor the lowest bidder
D. Intentionally vague specifications that allow multiple interpretations and increase disputes

questions.text: What is 'price fixing' in the procurement context?
options: A. Setting a fixed price in the contract to prevent cost overruns
B. Competitors agreeing in advance to offer predetermined prices to manipulate the bidding process
C. Adjusting prices based on market inflation indices
D. A vendor maintaining consistent pricing across multiple government contracts

questions.text: Which term describes the fraudulent practice where a contractor overstate the qualifications or prior experience of personnel assigned to a government contract?
options: A. Resume inflation
B. Staffing misrepresentation
C. Personnel substitution fraud
D. Experience certification fraud

questions.text: In contract fraud investigations, what is a 'kickback'?
options: A. A contractual penalty clause for non-performance
B. An unauthorized return of merchandise to the vendor for credit
C. A payment, benefit, or favor given to influence procurement decisions or contract awards
D. A price reduction retroactively applied to prior invoices

questions.text: What does 'time and materials fraud' specifically involve?
options: A. Falsifying the timeline of contract performance to claim penalty damages
B. Misreporting hours worked and inflating material costs on time-and-materials contracts
C. Delivering materials outside the specified timeframe to trigger contract penalties
D. Substituting lower-cost materials while billing for premium materials at agreed prices

questions.text: In procurement terminology, what is a 'split contract'?
options: A. Dividing contract work between two vendors to ensure quality control
B. Artificially dividing a single contract into multiple smaller contracts to avoid competitive bidding thresholds
C. Sharing contract revenue between the prime contractor and subcontractors
D. Splitting payment terms between upfront and milestone-based payments

questions.text: What is the defining characteristic of 'invoice splitting' in contract fraud?
options: A. Breaking down a single invoice into itemized line items for detailed accounting
B. Dividing contract deliverables among multiple vendors for parallel performance
C. Fragmenting a single invoice into multiple smaller invoices to bypass approval thresholds or hide the true transaction amount
D. Distributing payment responsibility across multiple cost centers or departments

questions.text: Which concept refers to a contractor providing false certifications regarding cost or pricing data in government contract proposals?
options: A. Cost concealment
B. Pricing misrepresentation
C. Defective pricing
D. Cost certification fraud

questions.text: In procurement fraud, what is 'warehousing' in the context of inventory?
options: A. Properly storing contracted materials in secure facilities per contract requirements
B. Purchasing and holding inventory in advance to qualify for bulk purchase discounts
C. Falsely billing for materials stored in inventory as if they were delivered to the contracting agency
D. Consolidating multiple vendor shipments into a single warehouse before final distribution

questions.text: What does the term 'contingent pricing' fraudulently involve in contract negotiations?
options: A. Setting prices that adjust based on actual performance metrics
B. Offering discounted pricing contingent upon the customer awarding additional future contracts
C. Pricing models that depend on meeting quality standards before final payment
D. Using competitor pricing as the baseline for determining contract price

questions.text: A contract manager at a government agency notices that a vendor consistently submits invoices for labor hours that exceed the contractually specified hourly limits, yet the work performed appears legitimate. Upon investigation, the CFE discovers the vendor is billing at inflated rates while falsifying timesheets to justify the higher amounts. Which fraudulent billing scheme best describes this practice?
options: A. Time and materials overstatement with rate manipulation
B. Progress payment acceleration fraud
C. Contingent liability billing
D. Overhead allocation shifting

questions.text: During a procurement audit, a CFE identifies that specifications for a construction supply contract were written to match only one vendor's product dimensions and technical capabilities, making competition impossible. However, the contract was not designated as sole source. What is the primary legal consequence of this action for the procuring organization?
options: A. Violation of competitive bidding requirements and potential contract voidability
B. Automatic fine under the False Claims Act
C. Criminal charges against the procurement officer
D. Mandatory rebidding with penalty deposits

questions.text: A subcontractor submits a proposal indicating it will source materials from a particular supplier at $50 per unit, but after contract award, it secretly sources identical materials from a different supplier at $30 per unit and retains the price difference without disclosure to the prime contractor or government. This best exemplifies which fraud scheme?
options: A. Unauthorized supplier substitution with profit retention
B. Preferred vendor kickback arrangement
C. Material cost allocation fraud
D. Supply chain omission scheme

questions.text: A CFE investigating a defense contractor discovers that the contractor certified compliance with cost accounting standards (CAS) but actually used different cost allocation methodologies in calculating proposed costs versus actual billing. Which regulatory framework was violated?
options: A. Cost Accounting Standards (CAS) consistency and flexibility requirements
B. Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP) only
C. Truth in Negotiations Act (TINA) disclosure requirements
D. Federal Acquisition Regulation (FAR) price adjustment clauses

questions.text: An equipment rental company includes inflated hourly rental rates in its contract proposal but justifies them as market rates. However, internal documents show the company's actual cost basis is 40% lower. The company is awarded the contract and bills accordingly. Which element distinguishes this from typical price inflation and makes it fraudulent?
options: A. Knowing misrepresentation of cost or pricing data at contract inception
B. Simple overcharging without fraudulent intent
C. Excessive profit margin within regulatory limits
D. Failure to update pricing during contract performance

questions.text: During contract administration, a CFE observes that a contractor routinely requests change orders for work that was arguably covered under the original contract scope. Each change order is individually small (under the approval threshold) but collectively represent 35% contract cost growth. What is this pattern most consistent with?
options: A. Scope creep exploitation through incremental change orders
B. Good faith contract interpretation differences
C. Normal contingency consumption
D. Differing site conditions adjustment

questions.text: A CFE is analyzing bids received for a government contract. Four bidders submitted proposals, but upon detailed review, three of the proposals contain identical mathematical errors in the cost calculations, all resulting in slightly higher final prices. Investigation reveals the bidders are competitors but have no direct contact. Which indicator most strongly suggests collusion versus coincidence?
options: A. The identical pattern of errors combined with historical knowledge of prior bid-fixing coordination
B. The fact that mathematical errors occurred in three proposals
C. The timing of bid submission was similar
D. The three companies operate in the same geographic region

questions.text: A contractor on a cost-plus-fixed-fee contract allocates a corporate overhead rate of 45% to the contract, significantly higher than its average 22% historical rate. When questioned, the contractor claims the specific contract required enhanced support services. No supporting documentation exists for the increased allocation. What audit concern is most critical here?
options: A. Unsubstantiated and potentially unallowable overhead allocation
B. Legitimate business decision regarding contract-specific resource allocation
C. Normal variance in overhead rates across different contract types
D. Appropriate reflection of increased complexity and risk

questions.text: A CFE discovers that a prime contractor on a government contract created a shell subcontracting company owned by the same individuals who own the prime. The shell subcontractor bills the prime at marked-up rates for services the prime's own employees could perform. Funds ultimately flow back to the prime's ownership. What is the primary fraud concern?
options: A. Fraudulent inducement through undisclosed conflict of interest and profit diversion
B. Legitimate subcontracting arrangement with transparent billing
C. Standard cost-sharing between affiliated entities
D. Allowable business structure for tax efficiency

questions.text: In reviewing a contract for professional services, a CFE notes that the contractor submitted a proposal based on specific labor classifications and billing rates. Upon performance, the contractor performed the work using junior personnel at lower actual costs but billed at the originally proposed senior-level rates. The contract lacks specific performance metrics or quality standards. Is this fraudulent?
options: A. Yes—misrepresentation regarding performance capability and resource allocation at contract inception
B. No—the contractor met deliverables within the contractual timeline
C. No—resource allocation decisions are contractor discretion on fixed-price contracts
D. Yes—violation of prevailing wage standards for all personnel

questions.text: A CFE investigating a construction contract discovers that the contractor submitted false progress certifications showing work 60% complete when only 35% was actually finished. These certifications triggered progress payments totaling $2.8 million. The contractor subsequently abandoned the project. Which fraud classification applies?
options: A. Progress payment fraud through false completion certifications
B. Contract breach with civil liability only
C. Legitimate contract dispute regarding milestone interpretation
D. Delay-based cost recovery claim

questions.text: A government contracting officer awards a contract to a vendor after competitive bidding. Six months into performance, the CFE learns that the winning bidder had a prior undisclosed business relationship with a technical evaluator on the source selection panel. The relationship ended three months before the evaluation. Does this constitute disqualifying conflict of interest?
options: A. Yes—failure to disclose creates appearance of bias and potential procurement irregularity
B. No—the relationship had ended before evaluation began
C. No—personal relationships do not constitute conflicts under federal procurement rules
D. Yes—only if the evaluator directly benefited financially during the procurement

questions.text: A CFE comparing two similar contracts with the same contractor notes that one contract (awarded through competitive bidding) has a unit price of $145, while another contract (awarded sole source citing urgency) has an identical unit price of $285 for the same item. The contractor claims the sole source contract required expedited delivery. Expedited delivery typically costs 8-12% premium. What is the most appropriate investigative conclusion?
options: A. Potential sole source abuse with price premium unjustified by expedited delivery requirements
B. Legitimate pricing differentiation based on delivery timeline and contract terms
C. Normal market fluctuation in commodity pricing
D. Appropriate cost of capital adjustment for faster performance

questions.text: During a procurement investigation, a CFE discovers that a vendor submitted identical bids across multiple contract opportunities with different government agencies, despite having different technical requirements and specifications. What fraud indicator does this most likely represent?
options: A. Collusive bidding with predetermined bid amounts
B. Cover bidding by a designated primary bidder
C. Bid suppression orchestrated by a competitor
D. Specification manipulation to favor the vendor

questions.text: A CFE is examining vendor invoices and notices that a supplier consistently bills the same labor rates for different skill levels across multiple contract lines. When cross-referenced with timesheets, the actual work performed shows significant variance in complexity. Which fraud scheme does this indicate?
options: A. Fictitious vendor fraud
B. Labor misclassification and overbilling
C. Invoice manipulation through duplicate submissions
D. Ghost employee scheme

questions.text: An organization's procurement manager has the authority to approve contracts under $50,000 without competitive bidding. A CFE discovers that the manager has been splitting a $120,000 software license contract into three separate $39,000 purchase orders, each processed by different vendors as intermediaries. What is the primary fraud concern?
options: A. Kickback scheme involving vendor intermediaries
B. Circumventing competitive bidding requirements through contract fragmentation
C. Fictitious vendor creation to launder organizational funds
D. Collusion between the procurement manager and all three vendors

questions.text: During a contract audit, a CFE identifies that a contractor submitted cost proposals based on historical pricing data, but upon contract award, the contractor's actual cost structure showed material differences not disclosed during negotiations. The contractor claims market conditions changed. What principle has likely been violated?
options: A. The Cost Accounting Standards (CAS) pricing consistency requirement
B. The duty of disclosure regarding known cost differences in certified cost or pricing data
C. The Prompt Payment Act requirement for transparent billing
D. The Federal Acquisition Regulation prohibition on sole source contracting

questions.text: A CFE investigating a construction contract discovers that the contractor invoiced for materials that were supposedly purchased from a supplier, but the supplier's records show no corresponding sales to the contractor. The contractor claims the materials were obtained through a local distributor. What red flag is most significant?
options: A. The invoice lacks proper vendor authorization codes
B. Inability to verify the documented supply chain and authenticity of material purchases
C. The contractor failed to provide quantity certifications on the invoice
D. The payment was made via wire transfer instead of check

questions.text: An organization uses a preferred vendor list to streamline procurement. A CFE discovers that contracts are routinely awarded to vendors on this list without price comparisons, and the list was created by a purchasing manager who has a family relationship with several vendors. What is the primary fraud risk?
options: A. Violation of open competition requirements and potential conflicts of interest
B. Automatic violation of the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act
C. Breach of the contractor's confidentiality obligations
D. Creation of a fictitious vendor network
questions.tag: Conflict of Interest in Vendor Selection

questions.text: During a contract review, a CFE notes that a vendor submitted a proposal with exceptionally low pricing compared to previous quotes from competitors. Upon investigation, the vendor admits to intentionally underpricing to secure the contract, planning to recover costs through inflated change orders. What does this represent?
options: A. Aggressive but legitimate competitive pricing strategy
B. Loss leader marketing tactic permitted under procurement law
C. Low-ball bidding with intent to inflate costs later through change orders
D. Normal market competition that should not trigger investigation

questions.text: A CFE examining subcontractor relationships on a federal construction contract discovers that the prime contractor requires all subcontractors to purchase materials exclusively from a specified supplier at marked-up prices, significantly above market rates. The prime contractor receives undisclosed rebates from this supplier. What fraud scheme is this?
options: A. Bid rotation among approved subcontractors
B. Kickback scheme combined with captive supplier arrangement
C. Legitimate volume discount arrangement
D. Acceptable cost-plus contracting methodology

questions.text: During an audit of a service contract, a CFE discovers that the contractor's timesheets show employees working on the contract during periods when the employees were actually on paid leave according to payroll records. The contractor invoiced full labor costs for these periods. What is this fraud categorized as?
options: A. Unallowable overhead allocation
B. Ghost employee fraud with falsified timesheets
C. Labor hour inflation through time record manipulation
D. Subcontractor misclassification

questions.text: A CFE is investigating a contractor's overhead allocation methodology and discovers that indirect costs are being charged to multiple government contracts simultaneously based on the same supporting documentation (e.g., a single facility rent invoice allocated to five different contracts). What is the primary fraud indicator?
options: A. Double-charging of overhead costs across multiple contracts
B. Legitimate cost allocation under standard accounting principles
C. Acceptable burden rate methodology
D. Proper use of cost accounting standards

questions.text: During a procurement investigation, a CFE discovers communications showing that competitors submitted bids on the same contract, but one competitor's bid was significantly higher with advance knowledge that they would lose. Later, that same competitor receives a lucrative sole source contract from the same buyer. What is most likely occurring?
options: A. Complementary bidding as part of a broader collusive arrangement
B. Coincidental business relationship between buyer and vendor
C. Legitimate differences in vendor capabilities and pricing
D. Normal competitive market dynamics

questions.text: A CFE examining a contract file discovers that contract terms were modified significantly after award through a series of informal amendments and email communications, none of which were formally documented or approved through the organization's change control process. The cumulative effect increased contract value by 35%. What compliance risk does this represent?
options: A. Failure to maintain audit trail and segregation of duties in contract management
B. Legitimate use of informal amendments common in industry practice
C. Acceptable cost management under accelerated project timelines
D. Proper documentation of contingency funding

questions.text: A CFE is reviewing a vendor's proposal and notices that the pricing structure includes line items that are identical in cost to line items in another vendor's proposal obtained during the due diligence process. When contacted, both vendors provide different explanations for their costs. What should the CFE's primary action be?
options: A. Award the contract to the vendor with the lower overall price
B. Investigate the possibility of collusion or information sharing between competitors
C. Reject both proposals as unreliable
D. Request both vendors reduce their bids by 10 percent

questions.text: A procurement manager at a government agency notices that three different vendors consistently submit bids that are nearly identical in price and structure, yet each vendor wins approximately one-third of the contracts issued annually. Upon deeper investigation, the CFE discovers evidence of pre-bid meetings between vendor representatives. Which fraud scheme is most likely occurring?
options: A. Bid rotation scheme with collusive market allocation
B. Complementary bidding with intentional overbidding
C. Specification tailoring to favor specific vendors
D. Subcontractor pass-through fraudulent markup

questions.text: During a contract audit, a CFE identifies that a contractor submitted cost proposals based on labor rates from a competitor that were obtained through an employee who previously worked for that competitor. The contractor won the contract by underbidding all rivals. Under what legal framework would this most likely constitute fraud?
options: A. Federal Acquisition Regulation (FAR) false statements clause only
B. Truth in Negotiations Act (TINA) due to misrepresentation of cost data accuracy
C. Sherman Act antitrust violations exclusively
D. Small Business Administration size standard misrepresentation
questions.tag: Cost Estimation Fraud & Competitive Intelligence Misuse

questions.text: A CFE investigating a construction contract notices that invoices submitted by the prime contractor include labor costs for work performed by subcontractors, but the subcontractors' own invoices to the prime are significantly lower. The difference represents 15% of total monthly billing. What is the most appropriate classification of this scheme?
options: A. Fictitious subcontractor fraud scheme
B. Double billing or duplicate invoice submission
C. Labor cost inflation and markup concealment
D. Material cost variance manipulation

questions.text: An organization's procurement audit reveals that a vendor was granted emergency sole source procurement authority due to claimed supply chain disruption. However, investigation shows the company deliberately failed to maintain adequate inventory levels to manufacture this justification. Which category of procurement fraud best describes this scheme?
options: A. Unauthorized sole source abuse through manufactured scarcity
B. Bid suppression via artificial market constraints
C. Specification manipulation for exclusionary purposes
D. Emergency procurement false pretense inflation

questions.text: A CFE discovers that a contractor systematically quotes higher prices when bidding on contracts containing detailed specifications, and lower prices when bidding on contracts with performance-based specifications. Investigation reveals the contractor maintains undisclosed relationships with specification writers. Which indicator most strongly suggests fraudulent scheme design?
options: A. Price variance correlating with specification type indicates competitive market response
B. Undisclosed relationships with specification writers combined with systematic pricing advantage suggests collusion
C. Performance-based contracts naturally attract lower bidding from qualified vendors
D. Higher pricing on detailed specifications reflects legitimate cost-accounting practices

questions.text: During contract reconciliation, a CFE identifies that a government contractor submitted certified cost or pricing data showing material costs of $50 per unit, but internal records reveal actual costs of $35 per unit obtained from the same supplier. The contractor claimed the difference reflected volume-based pricing tiers not yet achieved. Investigation confirms the contractor has never achieved those volumes. What is the primary fraud violation?
options: A. Unintentional cost estimation error under FAR guidelines
B. Defective pricing under Truth in Negotiations Act with certification breach
C. Legitimate forward-pricing adjustment for anticipated volume increases
D. Supplier-based pricing discrimination under antitrust law

questions.text: A CFE reviewing contract performance discovers that a vendor submitted invoices for 'equipment maintenance services' that were actually performed by the vendor's own internal staff, yet invoiced at the contracted subcontractor labor rate (35% higher than the vendor's standard labor rate). The contract explicitly prohibits markup on internal labor recharacterized as subcontractor work. What is the primary fraud mechanism?
options: A. Fictitious expense reimbursement scheme
B. Labor rate inflation through misclassification and prohibited markup
C. Unauthorized subcontracting abuse
D. Material cost substitution fraud

questions.text: An investigation reveals that a contractor submitted change order requests for scope additions that were actually included in the original contract specifications but deliberately omitted from its bid proposal. The contractor later claimed these were 'unforeseen site conditions' requiring change orders. What fraud classification applies?
options: A. Bid suppression through strategic scope concealment
B. Change order fraud via false scope addition claims
C. Specification misinterpretation as innocent bidding error
D. Cost overrun due to legitimate contract interpretation disagreement

questions.text: A CFE investigating a multi-year service contract discovers that the contractor substituted lower-grade materials in early contract performance without client authorization, but submitted invoices reflecting the higher-grade materials specified in the contract. In subsequent years, the contractor resumed using specified materials. What explains the pattern of selective fraud?
options: A. Initial quality control problems followed by contractor self-correction
B. Contractor testing lower-cost alternatives with client approval
C. Strategic product substitution fraud during initial contract phase to offset start-up costs
D. Supplier supply chain disruption requiring temporary material alternatives

questions.text: During a procurement investigation, a CFE identifies that a small business contractor, certified as disadvantaged for preferential contract award, had undisclosed control exercised by a large prime contractor through management services agreements, shared facilities, and guaranteed minimum purchases. The small business retained only nominal operational independence. What fraud classification applies?
options: A. Legitimate business relationship between prime and small business partners
B. Size standard fraud and small business program abuse through control circumvention
C. Preferential contracting justification based on business association agreements
D. Subcontracting arrangement properly disclosed under FAR requirements

questions.text: A CFE analyzing vendor payments discovers that a contractor submitted invoices using 'cost-plus-fixed-fee' contract terms but internally allocated overhead costs to the contract using a methodology that differed from the disclosed allocation base, resulting in 8% higher overhead charges. When confronted, the contractor provided documentation showing the alternative methodology was disclosed in a subsidiary cost accounting standards (CAS) disclosure statement. What is the legal position?
options: A. Contractor is compliant due to CAS disclosure documentation
B. Contractor committed defective pricing fraud despite subsidiary disclosure if primary proposal used different methodology
C. Contractor properly allocated costs under flexible cost accounting standards
D. No fraud exists because overhead allocation methodologies are contractor discretionary
questions.tag: Cost Accounting Standards & Overhead Allocation Fraud

questions.text: Investigation of a construction contract reveals the prime contractor systematically transferred portions of work to subcontractors not included in the original bid proposal, while retaining the original profit margin on the transferred work. The contract permitted subcontracting but required advance written approval for specific subcontractors. The prime obtained blanket subcontracting authorization without naming specific entities. What fraud scheme applies?
options: A. Legitimate subcontracting under blanket authorization granted by contract
B. Unauthorized subcontracting with kickback scheme for profit retention
C. Bid suppression through deliberate scope concealment at prime level
D. Subcontractor fraud through misrepresented work performance location

questions.text: A CFE examining vendor performance data discovers that a contractor's certified proposal included unit costs based on anticipated volume discounts from suppliers, contingent on achieving minimum annual purchases of $2M. The contract value was $1.8M annually. When challenged, the contractor provided evidence that supplier agreements did exist but were structured to allow pricing adjustments only if future multi-year contracts exceeded the minimum threshold. What is the fraud assessment?
options: A. Contractor committed defective pricing by including conditional discounts as certain in cost proposal
B. Contractor properly disclosed contingent pricing in good faith cost estimates
C. Contractor's reliance on anticipated future volume is standard industry practice
D. No fraud because supplier agreements technically existed at time of proposal submission
